---
title: "Lab 1: From Clicking to Coding – Database Exploration via API"
format: html
engine: knitr
filters:
  - webr
---

## Introduction

Biological databases are growing at an exponential rate. While manual searching via web interfaces is common, modern bioinformatics relies on automation. Today, we will learn how to bypass the graphical interface and retrieve data directly into our scripts using APIs (Application Programming Interface).



## Part 1: Warming up with R – Using APIs instead of Browsers

[cite_start]In the past, you might have searched for the human *APOE* gene (Accession: `NM_000041.4`) manually [cite: 310-312]. Now, we will do this using R code.

**Task 1:**
Run the code below to fetch the *APOE* gene sequence using the `rentrez` package.

```{webr-r}
# Loading packages (pre-installed in WebR)
webr::install("rentrez")
webr::install("seqinr")
library(rentrez)
library(seqinr)

# Fetching APOE sequence (Nuccore) in FASTA format
apoe_fasta <- entrez_fetch(db="nuccore", id="NM_000041.4", rettype="fasta")

# Display the result
cat(apoe_fasta)
```

**Discussion:**
* [cite_start]Change the database from `"nuccore"` to `"protein"` and try to fetch the protein sequence encoded by this gene (you'll need to find its *protein_id* [cite: 319]).
* Use the `seqinr` package to check the frequency of each nucleotide.

---

## Part 2: Introduction to Python and Biopython

Python is equally essential in bioinformatics. Instead of manually searching PubMed for publications by Piotr Zielenkiewicz, we will use the Biopython library.

### How to use Google Colab

Google Colab is a free Python environment that runs entirely in your browser — no installation required. All you need is a Google account.

1. Go to [colab.research.google.com](https://colab.research.google.com/).
2. Click **"New notebook"** in the top-left corner.
3. A blank notebook opens with one code cell. Run any cell with the ▶ button on the left or **Shift+Enter**.
4. Lines starting with `!` are system commands (e.g. installing packages via `pip`) — Colab runs them in a terminal.

::: {.callout-tip}
## Note: Colab resets between sessions
Every time you reopen a notebook, you must re-run the `!pip install` cell. Installed packages are not saved between sessions.
:::

### Task 2.1: Searching PubMed with Biopython

Copy the code below into a new cell in Google Colab and run it.

```python
!pip install biopython

from Bio import Entrez

# NCBI Good Practice - always provide your email!
Entrez.email = "your_email@student.pw.edu.pl"

# Searching the PubMed database
handle = Entrez.esearch(db="pubmed", term="Zielenkiewicz P[Author]")
record = Entrez.read(handle)

print(f"Number of publications found: {record['Count']}")
print(f"IDs of latest publications (PMIDs): {record['IdList']}")
```

**To do:** Modify the `term` variable to find the 1953 Nature papers by Watson and Crick.

### Task 2.2: Fetching a protein sequence in Python

Now fetch the same APOE sequence you retrieved in R — this time using Python.

```python
from Bio import Entrez, SeqIO

Entrez.email = "your_email@student.pw.edu.pl"

# Fetch the APOE protein sequence in FASTA format
handle = Entrez.efetch(db="protein", id="NP_000032.1", rettype="fasta", retmode="text")
sequence = SeqIO.read(handle, "fasta")

print(f"ID: {sequence.id}")
print(f"Sequence length: {len(sequence.seq)} amino acids")
print(f"First 50 aa: {sequence.seq[:50]}")
```

**Discussion:** Compare the result with what you saw in UniProt for `P02649`. Does the sequence length match?

---

## Part 3: Integration Project – Ebola Virus

Using the NCBI Taxonomy database, we can find sequences for the Ebola virus. Write a short R script (using the WebR editor above) to fetch the full genome of the Zaire Ebolavirus (find its Accession ID online) and calculate its **GC-content**.

---

## Part 4: Clinical Context – Osteosarcoma

Throughout this course, we will return to data from [osteosarc.com](https://osteosarc.com/) — a publicly available multi-omic dataset from an **osteosarcoma** patient. Osteosarcoma is a rare but aggressive bone cancer, most commonly affecting children and young adults.

Two genes play a central role in its development:

* **TP53** (`P04637`) — the "guardian of the genome", a tumor suppressor. TP53 mutations are found in over 50% of osteosarcoma cases.
* **RB1** (`P06400`) — originally discovered in retinoblastoma, but also critical in osteosarcoma.

### Task 4.1: Manual exploration in NCBI and UniProt

1. Go to [NCBI Gene](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/gene/) and search for `TP53`. Find the RefSeq accession numbers for the mRNA (`NM_...`) and protein (`NP_...`).
2. Go to [UniProt](https://www.uniprot.org/) and search for `P04637`. Compare the **Disease & Variants** section with what you saw earlier for APOE.
3. Go to [ClinVar](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/clinvar/) and search for `TP53`. How many clinical variants are described for this gene?

::: {.callout-note}
## ClinVar — the clinical variant database
ClinVar is an NCBI database that collects information about genetic variants and their clinical significance. Each variant is classified as *Pathogenic*, *Likely pathogenic*, *Benign*, or *Uncertain significance*.
:::

### Task 4.2: Fetching the TP53 sequence via API in R

```{webr-r}
install.packages("rentrez")
library(rentrez)

# Fetch the TP53 mRNA sequence in FASTA format
tp53_fasta <- entrez_fetch(db="nuccore", id="NM_000546.6", rettype="fasta", retmode="text")

# Display the header and beginning of the sequence
cat(substr(tp53_fasta, 1, 300))
```

### Task 4.3: TP53 protein from UniProt in R

```{webr-r}
# Fetch the TP53 protein sequence directly from the UniProt REST API
tp53_id <- "P04637"
url <- paste0("https://rest.uniprot.org/uniprotkb/", tp53_id, ".fasta")
sequence <- readLines(url)
cat(sequence, sep = "\n")
```

**Discussion:**

* Compare the length of the TP53 sequence to that of APOE from Task 1. Which protein is longer?
* Find the UniProt accession number for RB1 and fetch its sequence using the same code.
* Go back to ClinVar and check which TP53 variants are classified as *Pathogenic*. Do you see any mention of osteosarcoma?
